abstract:
The Village Institutes represent a cornerstone of educational reform during the early years of the Turkish Republic. Established in 1940 primarily to train rural teachers, the mission of these institutions was expanded in 1943 to address the nation's critical shortage of healthcare personnel. This led to the formation of the "Health Branch," which trained dedicated village health officers to work alongside teachers in enhancing the welfare of rural populations.

This study reconstructs the history and professional contributions of these officers through a combination of literature review and oral history. The authors conducted interviews with four surviving village health officers, the last of whom graduated in 1951. These first-hand accounts are supported by a rich collection of primary source documents provided by the interviewees, including student identification cards, period-specific medical textbooks, official diplomas, Sahra Sanitary Service School certificates, and specialization permits in anesthesia.

The research demonstrates that village health officers received a rigorous, high-level education tailored specifically to the needs of rural Anatolia. Armed with both theoretical knowledge and practical skills, they played a vital role in local public health by managing infectious diseases, improving sanitation, and performing minor medical procedures. By bringing these neglected figures to the forefront, the study sheds light on a significant era of Turkish health policy and educational philosophy, illustrating how integrated social services were once envisioned to transform rural society.
